Sorry for the my late reaction. I finally release version 3.0.2 to github and [pypi](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/ldif3/3.0.2).

> My motivation behind this is that the author of python-ldap asked Debian to remove the package and I would like to package ldif3 as a LDIF-Reader/Writer replacement in Debian.

I have thought a bit about this in the last weeks. I feel honored that you consider this project a useful replacement. But I am not totally sure if this is a good idea. A big issue is that this library is not backwards compatible with python-ldap.

I feel like the python-ldap community is aware of the issue of python3 compatibility and there are several people working on that. For example, [rbarrois/python-ldap](https://github.com/rbarrois/python-ldap/tree/py3) has a py3 branch. I just don't feel like I am the most competent of these people.

You can of course package this project for debian and I would be glad to get further contributions (e.g. bug reports). But please check if this is really your best option.

I think we can (and should) figure out how to adjust the content handlers concept to make this work.

At the moment a content handler is engage when processing the `data` key if it "accepts" the content-type expressed in the request headers. That calls `dumps` which is responsible for returning a string or byte string that is _the entire_ body of the request that will be made. We want to preserve that interface.

The challenge with this for `multipart` is that `dumps` has no way of changing or reading the `content-type` request header in order to build a body with the proper boundaries. `dumps` is currently a staticmethod.

If we allowed dumps to accept an optional `test` param, then it could have access to the other test data and thus either change the content-type or read it to set the boundary.

This would then allow an external multipart content handler package to be created which does whatever it needs to do.

Would that work? There's a lot in your message above that I don't actually understand or is way more complex than I was imagining things, so it seems likely I'm missing a critical detail. For example you seem to be imaging some way in which each "part" would get its own tests. That's not the granularity that gabbi wants to work at. Gabbi makes single requests and then makes assertions on single responses, in sequence.

(BTW, I've not forgotten about the coerce stuff, just very busy with the day job.)

Ok, I felt really bad by neglecting these issues, and every time that I thought about it, that idea pushed me away from going back to work on pew once again :/

Now I finally booted into windows to dig into this issue.

Good news: pew has worked inside `babun` all along.

Bad news: this applies only to a python installed inside cygwin.

This is the [`CreateProcess`](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms682425%28v=vs.85%29.aspx?f=255&MSPPError=-2147217396) I was talking about. Python's [subprocess](https://docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess.html) uses `popen` on posix systems, and `CreateProcess` on windows. So what I meant earlier is that the error was due to a `subprocess.call` or `subprocess.check_call`, and the error message was Windows specific.

Basically, the problem you've found is because `SHELL` is set to `/bin/zsh`, but `CreateProcess` is unable to find `/bin/zsh` (obviously, since that's not a valid Windows path)

You can work around it with `env SHELL=zsh pew workon envname` or `pew in envname zsh`

But then you'll stumble another error... pew will use `sys.executable` (that is the path of the Python interpreter itself). The problem now is the opposite: this is a Windows path and it'll be supplied as an argument to `SHELL` (zsh in this case) which doesn't treat `\` as the file separator.

I tried to make a simple conversion with `Path(sys.executable).as_posix()` but this will yield `C:/yada/yada` which won't still be accepted by zsh due to the leading drive specifier.

You can see the difference in behavior between the 2 pythons here:

    { ~ }  » /usr/bin/python                                                                        ~
    Python 2.7.8 (default, Jul 28 2014, 01:34:03)
    [GCC 4.8.3] on cygwin
    Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
    >>> import os
    >>> os.path.sep
    '/'
    >>> from subprocess import call
    >>> call(['/bin/zsh', '-c', 'echo 1'])
    1
    0
    >>> exit()
    [1]    6228 abort (core dumped)  /usr/bin/python
    { ~ }  » /cygdrive/c/tools/python2/python -i                                                ~ 134
    Python 2.7.8 (default, Jun 30 2014, 16:03:49) [MSC v.1500 32 bit (Intel)] on win32
    Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
    >>> import os
    >>> os.path.sep
    '\\'
    >>> from subprocess import call
    >>> call(['/bin/zsh'])
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
      File "C:\tools\python2\lib\subprocess.py", line 522, in call
        return Popen(*popenargs, **kwargs).wait()
      File "C:\tools\python2\lib\subprocess.py", line 710, in __init__
        errread, errwrite)
      File "C:\tools\python2\lib\subprocess.py", line 958, in _execute_child
        startupinfo)
    WindowsError: [Error 2] The system cannot find the file specified
    

Maybe that could be fixed with something like [what mingw is doing](http://www.mingw.org/wiki/Posix_path_conversion). Furthermore, I think that if you don't actually need the whole Posix API, but you only want an unix-like shell, mingw is a simpler solution.

Another solution could be to change the way the the shell_check is done, but I don't like the idea of making more code that is shell-specific. At the same time, I don't feel like adding kludges to fix this, guessing the correct format for the path that we're dealing with, etc.

Especially when pew is already working correctly on a normal Windows environment and a Python installed on that environment, and on a Posix environment and a Python installed on that environment.

So, what you might want to do is install pew inside cygwin. Urllib3 complains about the TLS certificates stuff on Python2.7.8.
Since cygwin only currently ships that old (and [insecure](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0466/)) version of Python2, you might want to install Python3. You also need pip (actually, just an updated setuptools) at least until I won't have relaxed the setuptools requirement in `pythonz-bd`

    pact install python3-setuptools
    /usr/bin/easy_install-3.4 pip
    /usr/bin/pip3 install pew

and voila (you might need to `rebaseall` your cygwin, if you'll stumble on some [`address space needed by 'whatever.dll' (0x960000) is already occupied` error](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/11107155/how-to-fix-address-space-is-already-occupied-error-on-fetch-commit))


I'll leave this issue open for now, in case there'll be another idea... otherwise I'll just document these steps for installing pew inside cygwin.

More evidence that a Quantity is mutable, due to in-place operations that change the fundamental quantity (not just the representation via a unit conversion, which could be avoided by hashing a quantity represented in canonical (base) units).

The in-place operators are:
iadd_sub(quantity), ito(unit), ito_base_units(), and the dunder methods __iadd__, __isub__, __imul__, __idiv__, __ifloordiv__, __itruediv__, __ipow__

>>> pint.__version__
'0.4'
>>> ureg = pint.UnitRegistry()
>>> q = 10 * ureg.mm
>>> q
<Quantity(10, 'millimeter')>
>>> id(q)
140276778417232
>>> q += 1*ureg.mm
>>> q
<Quantity(11, 'millimeter')>
>>> id(q)
140276778417232
This is a tough call because it has to be unambiguous. Maybe we can open this for a wider debate and hear opinions.

The metaclass for PintType should register Quantity, as this is what is expected to be returned when doing a ExtensionArray[item] 

    @property
    def type(self):
        # type: () -> type
        """The scalar type for the array, e.g. ``int``
        It's expected ``ExtensionArray[item]`` returns an instance

https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/master/pandas/core/dtypes/base.py

This makes me believe pandas is expecting an array of scalar items, so for quantities that'd be 
[<Quantity(1, 'meter')>,
 <Quantity(2, 'meter')>,
 <Quantity(2, 'meter')>,
 <Quantity(3, 'meter')>]
(this is also how I've got _construct_from_sequence to work)
Which leaves several immediately obvious ways to create an Extensionarray, 
sequence of scalar quantities
vector quantity
matrix quantity (not allowed as Extensionarray are only 1d)
scalar quantity (acceptable? I haven't been dealing with this yet)

re construct_from_string comment
I think this is used to allow pandas to interpret a column as a pinttype when doing pd.read_csv(... dtype={"a":"pint"}. Can't find what I read to make me think this though!
I think there's the potential to to have an extensiontype for every unit, which will allow pandas to read a column with dtype={"columnname":"meter" and create an extensionarray directly.

re numpy
I don't think it's an issue having a numpy dependency as pandas needs it.

I see the following potential solution:

1. Add a new property to Base called `call_number` the defaults to `None`
2. In `getCall`, fill the property `call_number` with the inputted index `n`
3. In `args`, use `call_number` to only return the correct item from the `args_list`

Status is that I completely forgot about it after the 1.8.1 release. Instead I started (and almost completed) a refactoring of all the backends and their test cases, which clashes with your patches. Regarding the patch, I see the benefits of using jinja and I'm happy to introduce it as a dependency. Just some details:

1. I'm not convinced about the is_*_file additions to edatool.py since some backends need explicit handling of which vhdl version is in use etc, and isim needs to treat vlog2005 as sv files, but I guess they can stay.

2. I'm moving all backends towards a model where the configure stage creates a makefile and the build stage just runs `make`. As an extra bonus for the ise backend I introduced an intermediate target so it's easy to just create the project and then open it in the GUI to do the build. I want to do the same for Vivado.

```
picture.value = textbox.value
```

```
led.value = button.value
```

I found an alternative solution using `scipy.ndimage`, which solves my particular problem. However, I'm not sure whether the solution is general enough to be included in `trimesh`. What I ended up doing was using the matrix associated with the sparse_surface and filled in the solid part using morphological operations available in scipy:

```python
import trimesh
from trimesh.voxel import matrix_to_marching_cubes, sparse_to_matrix
from scipy import ndimage

mesh = trimesh.load('nasal_cavity.ply')

# get discretized surface 
mesh_discretized = mesh.voxelized(pitch=0.5)
matrix_surface = sparse_to_matrix(mesh_discretized.sparse_surface)

# fill the volume
matrix_full = ndimage.morphology.binary_fill_holes(matrix_surface)

# export using marching cubes
meshed = matrix_to_marching_cubes(matrix=matrix_full, pitch=mesh_discretized.pitch, origin=mesh_discretized.origin)
meshed.export('nasal_cavity_voxels.ply')
```

This procedure seems to be more robust than the available `fill_voxelization`, but it might fail miserably when the actual surface voxelization is not closed. One improvement could be to use only a nearest-neighbor connectivity in `binary_fill_holes`:

```python
kernel = ndimage.morphology.generate_binary_structure(3, 1)
matrix_full = ndimage.morphology.binary_fill_holes(matrix_surface, kernel)
```

> Since the only call to plt.anything is plt.gcf, could we just reproduce the contents of plt.gcf instead of importing pyplot at all?

No, I think we want the import here.  The idea is that if a user does not provide axes or a figure object, then they must be using the pyplot API.  If the call into our display code is the first pyplot thing the user has done, then no backend will have yet been selected.  However, we really want that to happen before executing any draw commands so that the state is updated appropriately.  (This solution was arrived at after conferring with the mpl devs in the context of librosa, but I think the general idea carries over.)

It's also bad form to access other packages' internals, so there's that.

It's less about having a deep/wide hierarchy of exceptions than making it easy to catch all mir_eval-generated exceptions in one go, if the caller wants to.  Right now, we can't distinguish mir_eval errors from numpy errors, or anything else.

I'm developing a tool that track processes that are running with outdated mmap'ed libraries (containing bugs or security issues or even a malware infected file using this to hide). /proc/pid/smap flags them with appending " (deleted)" at the end of the path.

Here's my thoughts:

1. I could add an option to `notifyOnMessage()` to tell it to skip the first batch of messages. However, this concept only applies to `getUpdates()` and is not applicable to webhooks, because webhooks don't receive updates batch by batch. Webhooks get pushed messages by Telegram servers continuously. It is impossible to say which messages are "initially present" and which are not. There is nothing wrong with an option that applies only to some selected circumstances, but in this case, I think the benefit is too small to justify the asymmetry.

2. I could add an option to `notifyOnMessage()` to tell it to ignore messages before a certain time. This would apply to both `getUpdates()` and webhooks, but another asymmetry happens. `InlineQuery` and `ChosenInlineResult` do not have a time; only normal chat `Message` does. Again, I could apply the time criteria to `Message` only. But again, I think the benefit is too small to justify the trouble.

Therefore, I have decided telepot will not touch this problem. You will have to take care of it yourself.

I missed your point, sorry.


The correct way to seed is something like this:

``` 
from randomgen import RandomGenerator, MT19937
rg = RandomGenerator(MT19937(seed))
```

An alternative is to use if you want to reseed

```
mt = MT19937(seed)
rg = mt.generator
mt.seed(reseed)
```

`RangomGenerator` has a pass-through seed implementation that I haven't figured out what to do with. 

```
g = rg.RandomGenerator()

In [3]: g.seed
Out[3]: <function RandomGenerator.seed>

In [4]: g.seed?
Docstring:
Reseed the basic RNG.

Parameters depend on the basic RNG used.
```

One final way

```
from randomgen import RandomGenerator, MT19937
rg = RandomGenerator(MT19937(seed))
rg._basicrng.seed(seed)
```

As an aside,

I'm quite keen to make requests a formal dependency of py3status, this would allow us to add a `Py3.get_url()` style function that could deal with all the timeouts etc to simplify things for the modules whilst ensuring robustness.  Doing this might also allow us to move some of these settings like timeouts out of the modules configs and be more general (they could still be used in specific modules eg like colors are).

I think the SSL support is an interesting thing and could be useful but it is important to keep modules configs separate from library specifics.  Ultimately requests is just a wrapper for the likes of urllib.

Yeah, at least the asynchronous modules should not be stopped. A thin py3status main process, which forks of the actual worker and prints data sounds like a good idea. Another possibility would be to centralize the event processing into another process. But then this process would need to buffer incoming events when the receiving modules are stopped - not sure whether this would be a viable solution.

I agree with @ultrabug on the dependency stuff.  Vendoring just seems to be an acceptance that python packaging is a mess and feels like a defeatist attitude that is bug prone and user abusive.  It makes requests no longer seem the nice project I believed it to be.  I'm also not a great fan of six

Anyhow back to this issue.

I think an `get_response_from_url()` that returns an object or throws an exception is a good thing.  I'm not sure we need so many exceptions but maybe that's fine they should all be subclassed anyhow so not a big deal.   It would have a few fancy methods to get the json, http return code etc.

However I still strongly think that py3 also wants a helper `get_json_from_url()` that will just wrap `get_response_from_url()` and only return json or None (it would silence all exceptions).  I think this is the most useful for actual module writers but  `get_response_from_url()` gives them a richer object if the want especially if they care about exceptions, otherwise all client modules need to be doing exception handling that most will ignore.

Anyhow I think this all makes for interesting discussion topics.
For this PR I'd be happy for `get_response_from_url()` to not actually be implemented fully and kept private as it will need to be really robust.

Also back to the exceptions.  They want to be available inside `Py3` maybe namespaced eg `Py3.exceptions.HttpConnectionError` currently this PR has `from py3status.py3 import URLError` and I don't think a module should ever do `import py3status.xxx`.

import happybase

connection = happybase.Connection('HOSTNAME_OF_THRIFT_SERVER', use_kerberos=True)

                      'family:qual2': 'value2'})

row = table.row('row-key')

for key, data in table.rows(['row-key-1', 'row-key-2']):
    print key, data  # prints row key and data for each row

I've pushed a fix for the 1900 `datetime.date()` issue to the master branch.

However, it is still broken for `datetime.date()`.  I'm going to document it here so I remember the next time.

From the updated code:

```python
    # The following is a workaround for the fact that in Excel a time only
    # value is represented as 1899-12-31+time whereas in datetime.datetime()
    # it is 1900-1-1+time so we need to subtract the 1 day difference.

```

This is yet another quirk in the way Excel handles dates around its 1900 epoch. Basically, although the default Excel Epoch is 1900 the 0 date is actually 1899-12-31 which is displayed as 1900-1-0 (which you saw in the bug report). 

Dates in Excel are stored as numbers so the following are the internal representation of some dates and times:

    1899-12-31 = 0
    00:00:00   = 0
    1900-1-1   = 1

So a time value only is represented as `1899-12-31 + time`

Whereas with `datetime.datetime()` we get:

```python
>>> datetime.datetime.strptime("00:00:00.0", "%H:%M:%S.%f")
datetime.datetime(1900, 1, 1, 0, 0)

>>> datetime.datetime.strptime("1900-1-1", "%Y-%m-%d")
datetime.datetime(1900, 1, 1, 0, 0)
```

I.e., `1900-1-1 + time`.

So XlsxWriter subtracts 1 day to map between the datetime.datetime time and the Excel time. However, that breaks the datetime.datetime date only value of 1900-1-1 since, as can be seen from the repl output above there is no way to distinguish between 1900-1-1 and 00:00:00.

That is a compromise for but I don't see any way around it for now.

Are you sure you have read the [docs](http://architect.readthedocs.org/features/partition/index.html), because it's all there, you missed several steps from the docs that is why you don't see any difference in  your database structure.

Here is a summary:

1) Create a Django model and run ``syncdb`` to create the table in the database
2) Add ``architect.install`` decorator to the model
3) Run the following command ``export DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=mysite.settings`` where mysite.settings is your settings module
4) Finally, run the following command ``architect partition --module path.to.the.model.module`` that will create the needed triggers in the database

That's it

Adding  `__init__.py` in the top-level source directory is not the right way to handle this.  For a tiny module such as `wavio`, I don't recommend using a git submodule.  The python module is a single file, `wavio.py`, and--although I did make a small change today--I don't expect it to change much.  It's fine with me if you simply copy the file and check it in as part of your project.

Another approach would be for me to put `wavio` on PyPI.  Then you could add it to your project dependencies.  I haven't done that, because I've thought of this code as a temporary work-around to scipy's lack of support for 24 bit files.  The code started out as an answer to a question on stackoverflow, and then spent a long time as a gist on github.  It would probably still be a gist, but after making a few changes to it, I realized the github API for a real repo was much nicer.  *Eventually* scipy should support 24 bit files, and `wavio` will become obsolete.  In the meantime, I'm fine with anyone simply copying the main file, `wavio.py`, into their own projects.

If you prefer to maintain the git submodule, you could hack `sys.path` (not recommended), or add some code to your `setup.py` script to install `wavio` into a directory that is in the search path.
This isn't that easy since the underlying concurrent.futures.Executor interface has no way of setting a timeout.
Adding native coroutine support is as far as I'm willing to go for this. I tried to support generator based coros too but it was too painful.

Thanks for putting this together. There are some good points.

I don't think that the default file storage has a default expiration time,
since it just stores files on disk. The HTTP cache headers are up to
whatever web server is used to cache these files. S3 is different in that
it requires you to choose the cache headers as you upload the file, rather
than having it be configurable on a per-bucket basis.

I think that, starting from scratch, a conservative default would be best.
However, the default expiration time has been 1 year since forever, and
changing it now will affect existing projects. Given that S3 hardcodes this
setting on each uploaded file, this would mean existing projects that
updated naively would end up with some files having 1 year expiry, and
other files having 1 hour expiry. When they notice this, changing the
setting back in their project, and re-syncing the changes across the entire
bucket, can be a very time-consuming operation.

I guess a way to do this in a backwards-compatible way would be to have a
warning emitted if the setting is not explicitly set, or to make a new
storage class with a different default. The former is going to be quite
noisy on the console, and the latter is an ongoing source of maintenance
and confusion for people.

So, while I agree this would have been a better way to go from scratch, I
think it's a bit damaging to make the change now.

Fundamentally, switching to the manifest storage fixes all these problems.


On 6 August 2017 at 04:25, Kevin Christopher Henry <notifications@github.com
> wrote:

> I'd like to suggest some changes to the way django-s3-storage handles the
> caching of static files.
>
> The first issue is that the default expiration time—1 year—is too long.
> That default applies not just to the versioned ManifestStaticS3Storage
> files but to the regular StaticS3Storage files as well. By comparison,
> django-storages doesn't set the header at all, by default, and whitenoise
> uses <http://whitenoise.evans.io/en/stable/django.html#WHITENOISE_MAX_AGE>
> either 0 or 60 seconds, depending on DEBUG.
>
> It's important to set a conservative default here because once a user's
> browser has cached a file there's no way for the developer to force it to
> refresh. So someone who starts with the default value and then decides to
> change it later will find that it's too late for some users.
>
> The second issue is that ManifestStaticS3Storage doesn't distinguish
> between the versioned (e.g. myfile.123456.css) and the unversioned (
> myfile.css) files when it comes to caching. The way this should work is
> that the versioned files get cached forever, while the unversioned files
> get cached for a relatively short time. whitenoise gets this right
> <https://github.com/evansd/whitenoise/blob/8c16bbccfcd771d0f434d11a6609c95a93323102/whitenoise/base.py#L206>,
> using the filename to figure out if it's a versioned file or not and
> setting the expiration time accordingly.
>
> So my suggestions are:
>
>    1. Lower the default expiration time (perhaps to one hour, to match
>    the file storage default, or lower).
>    2. With ManifestStaticS3Storage, set the expiration time differently
>    depending on whether or not it's a versioned file. If it is, set it to
>    cache forever. If it isn't, use the AWS_S3_MAX_AGE_SECONDS_STATIC
>    setting.
>

import PySimpleGUI as sg
import os

# Simple Image Browser based on PySimpleGUI

# Get the folder containing the images from the user
rc, folder = sg.GetPathBox('Image Browser', 'Image folder to open', default_path='X:/- EBAY - Gruen/Images - Oct Nov Dec 2017')

if len(png_files) == 0:
    sg.MsgBox('No PNG images in folder')
    exit(0)

# create the form that also returns keyboard events
form = sg.FlexForm('Image Browser', return_keyboard_events=True, location=(0,0), use_default_focus=False )

# make these 2 elements outside the layout because want to "update" them later
# initialize to the first PNG file in the list
image_elem = sg.Image(filename=png_files[0])
filename_display_elem = sg.Text(png_files[0], size=(80, 3))
file_num_display_elem = sg.Text('File 1 of {}'.format(len(png_files)), size=(15,1))

# define layout, show and read the form
col = [[filename_display_elem],
          [image_elem],
          [sg.ReadFormButton('Next', size=(8,2)), sg.ReadFormButton('Prev', size=(8,2)), file_num_display_elem]]

col_files = [[sg.Listbox(values=filenames_only, size=(60,30), key='listbox')],
             [sg.ReadFormButton('Read')]]
layout = [[sg.Column(col_files), sg.Column(col)]]
button, values = form.LayoutAndRead(layout)          # Shows form on screen

# loop reading the user input and displaying image, filename
i=0
while True:

    # perform button and keyboard operations
    if button is None:
        break
    elif button in ('Next', 'MouseWheel:Down', 'Down:40', 'Next:34') and i < len(png_files)-1:
        i += 1
    elif button in ('Prev', 'MouseWheel:Up', 'Up:38', 'Prior:33') and i > 0:
        i -= 1

    if button == 'Read':
        filename = folder + '\\' + values['listbox'][0]
        # print(filename)
    else:
        filename = png_files[i]

    # update window with new image
    image_elem.Update(filename=filename)
    # update window with filename
    filename_display_elem.Update(filename)
    # update page display
    file_num_display_elem.Update('File {} of {}'.format(i+1, len(png_files)))

    # read the form
    button, values = form.Read()

First of all, you have to monkey patch the standard library so that it plays nice with the coroutine model of eventlet (if you haven't done that already). Just call `eventlet.monkey_patch()` at the very start of the application, before all your other imports.

The second problem is that you are never returning control back to the framework when the server receives the `handshake` event. Because eventlet is a coroutine framework, it depends on all the tasks releasing the CPU. Your `handshake()` function calls `startStreaming` and never returns or releases the CPU, so no other coroutine will be able to run, which means that no other messages will go in or out.

A quick hack that may allow you to start sending and receiving messages is to add a `sleep(0)` inside the for loop in `startStreaming()`. Each time you sleep, it gives other coroutines the opportunity to run.

A much much better solution, though, would be to have the `handshake()` function run `startStreaming()` as a background task, so that it can return immediately without blocking the framework.

I think you are confused by the `act_data` argument in the `Activation` example. This is a key/value argument that is used to bind the symbol with the data. Because in the example the Activation symbol is named `act`, the default input symbol should be named `act_data`

```
    y = test_utils.simple_forward(op, data0=x)
```

@mistercrunch You mean.. to disable a pool across multiple DAGs in order to indicate that a resource is down? I can see that use-case. 

However, It's hacky. I propose a different approach :  a UI control called "Enable/Disable Pool" backed by a new boolean column in the pool table and a check in the scheduler for this case.  The problem with setting the value from N to 0 is remembering what N was when you need to reset it.

Personally, I like the functionality and see it as valuable; I just question the semantics. The builtin `dict` type has both `get` and `setdefault` methods; the former gets a value or returns a default if the key is not present (without altering the dict) and the latter does the same but alters the dict to contain the default value.

Making a new, `setdefault` method on `Variable` parallels the semantics for the python standard library (thus sticking to the least surprise principle):

https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#dict.get
https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#dict.setdefault

This is expected behavior. The reason is that execution state is not possible to transfer between processes. If a new worker or process wants to resume there's no way. So they just restart from scratch. That's why tasks need to be idempotent for it to work.

We could potentially optimize it so that there's no reason to restart in case it's the same worker that resumes it, but that's a heuristic optimization.

@elpaisa Yep, for what it's worth the `URLLib3Session` is not an exact replacement for the old `BotocreHTTPSession` as their interfaces are quite different. The upgrade notes do mention that we're no longer using requests (which the old BotocoreHTTPSession inherited from). In general, if the class is not a part of our [public documentation](https://botocore.amazonaws.com/v1/documentation/api/latest/index.html), it's considered internal and shouldn't be used as we make no guarantees about their interfaces (or even presence).

This is certainly a bug, although I'm not entirely sure if it is a bug in botocore or a bug in the services, but I believe that either botocore should do this normalization when generating the signature, or the services should not.

FWIW, my revised function using the P.R. that @aaltat created:

```python
def get_column_number(table_locator, col_text, loglevel='INFO'):
    """== Returns Number of Specified Column ==
    
    Returns the number of the first column found which contains the ``col_text`` string.

    - Does not require an exact match; if there is a column named ``Foobar`` and you input ``Foo`` then it will match.
    
    === Calling ===
    
    | *Args* | ``table_locator`` (str) | The table locator containing the column whose number you desire. |
    |        | ``col_text`` (str) | The string of the column you want to locate. |
    | *Returns* | ``int`` | Number of column containing string ``col_name``. |
    | *Raises* | AssertionError | If ``col_name`` cannot be found in the table specified. |

    === Example in Robot ===

    | ${column_number} =    Get Column Number    xpath=//*[@id="deviceListHeader"]    Unsafe
    """
    sel2lib = _get_sel2lib()
    found_it = False
    counter = 0
    table = sel2lib.table_element_finder.find(table_locator)
    if table:
        head_cols = table.find_elements_by_xpath("./thead/tr/th")
        for header_col in head_cols:
            counter += 1
            element = sel2lib.element_finder.find(header_col)
            if col_text in element.text:
                output = counter
                found_it = True
                break
    else:
        sel2lib.log_source(loglevel)
        raise AssertionError("No table using selector '{}' found on the current page.".format(table_locator))
    if found_it is False:
        sel2lib.log_source(loglevel)
        raise AssertionError("No column containing '{}' found in the table identified via {}!".format(col_text, table_locator))
    return output
```

@dvzrv Allow me to explian. @techalchemy was trying to tell you that Pipenv does not actively enforce setuptools, pip, and wheel in the virtualenv it manages. What you provided were indirect evidence, e.g. “no-one else enforece them, so it must be Pipenv that did”. This can, however, be disproved by direct evidence, i.e. the source code of Pipenv, and as maintainers of this project, we can tell you with reasonable certainty that Pipenv does not pin setuptools to 39.2.0. Direct evidence trumps directict evidence, and in general can only be disproved by direct evidence—i.e. you need to find exactly where and how Pipenv pins setuptools.

To draw a bad analogy—my neighbour accouses my dog for stealing things from his garden yesterday. I don’t think she did. How can I prove it? I can’t, since I would have nothing to give if she didn’t take anything to begin with. My neighbour would either need to catch him doing it, or find the thing belong to him in my house. It doesn’t help if he said “well *my* dog didn’t take it, it must be yours”.

I could throw an exception if python <3.6 (in my case) is used, but it just makes it cumbersome for the user. If the user has a compatible python version installed, it would be better for him/her if pipenv used it.

I do think that this is something that will happen with more than a fair share of projects, and handling it directly within pipenv would avoid repetition. It could be the same as when one specifies a given version, and pipenv complains that it can’t find the version that is being requested, just that it would say that it can’t find any compatible version.

It would move the exception handling up the tool chain.

For now local exception it is, but, as I said, would be awesome (at least for me) if it was handled by pipenv.

My 2c from maintaining some [pre-commit](http://pre-commit.com/) [hooks](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks/blob/7539d8bd1a00a3c1bfd34cdb606d3a6372e83469/hooks.yaml#L132-L137) that deal with requirements.txt

We maintain several tools which make dealing with application requirements easier:

- A pre-commit hook (linked above) which sorts requirements to make merge conflicts much less likely
- A git merge-tool (sorry, closed source for now) which can automatically resolve conflicts in requirements
- Some additional (will hopefully be OS soon!) tools which assert pinning, encourage pruning, and assist in upgrading.

For the most part, these tools would be easy to adapt to the new lockfile proposed as it is an easy to parse format (json) in a regular structure (and in an ideal world, probably obsoletes the sorting hook since pip would only generate sorted json in a regular structure).

In the current world, these tools are very simple, mostly owing that to the dead simple nature of requirements.txt files (easy to parse, easy to manipulate, syntax is _dead simple_).

As soon as we move to a python syntax (or python-like syntax) a number of issues arise:
- There is no standard library way to round-trip parse and rewrite syntax (lib2to3 comes to mind but falls short for [a](https://github.com/bukzor/RefactorLib/issues/29) [number](https://github.com/bukzor/RefactorLib/issues/6) [of](https://github.com/bukzor/RefactorLib/issues/30) [reasons](https://github.com/bukzor/RefactorLib/issues/23) and has lead to a [bunch](https://github.com/google/yapf) [of](https://github.com/davidhalter/jedi) [different](https://github.com/habnabit/awpa) [implementations](https://github.com/hhatto/autopep8) of ast rewriting, all seemingly with different goals and issues).
- Automatic reordering is not necessarily a safe operation due to arbitrary code (nor possible?)

I think the `wheel` format made some great decisions (and really should be a strong precedent) on not making things executable.  When comparing to setup.py, environment specifiers over arbitrary code execution keeps metadata as just that, data.

We need this feature. Many projects already have many requirements files, more than two (main and dev) as in current Pipfile realization. Examples:

+ [zulip](https://github.com/zulip/zulip/tree/master/requirements)
+ [CarToPy](https://github.com/SciTools/cartopy/tree/master/requirements)
+ [librabbitmq](https://github.com/celery/librabbitmq/tree/master/requirements)
+ [mu-editor](https://github.com/mu-editor/mu) (have separated requirements for Raspberry Pi)

@virajpandya It is doing the correct calculation.  `calc_total_error` requires the `data` and `bkg_error` inputs to have the same units (as described above) and the output is also returned in the same units, **not** electrons.


This is the total error in *electrons* (assuming `data` and `bkg_error` have the same units):
The first term is the image Poisson statistics, which require the actual counts (e.g. electrons).

```
error_total [e-] =  np.sqrt((data * gain) + (bkg_error * gain)**2)
```

But we want to convert this result from electrons back to the native `data` and `bkg_error` units.  To do this we need to divide by the gain (electrons/ image_units):

```
error_total [image_units] = error_total [e-] / gain [e-/image_units]
```
giving:

```
error_total [image_units] = np.sqrt(data / gain + bkg_error**2)
```

This is what `calc_total_error` is correctly returning.

This problem have been solved in 1.0.0aX. Records module now never touches the JSON being stored in anyway. It's the responsibility of the one creating the record to include any recid/control numbers etc into the JSON (some PIDStore minters do this for you).

Each record is now identified by a UUID (not stored in the JSON). This UUID can either be generated by the one creating the record or automatically generated by the underlying model:

```python
# Set by you
Records.create({'title': 'My title'}, id_=uuid.uuid4())
# Automatically set by records:
Records.create({'title': 'My title'})
```

If a UUID provided by the user already exists, the record creation will fail with an IntegrityError.

hmm, in that case what about basing on `object` instead of `unicode` especially for `Namespace`, but potentially for all rdflib classes (esp. URIRef, Literal, BNode)?

I mean there is a certain convenience in basing on `unicode` as it allows to just put them wherever a `unicode`/`str` object is expected, but to be honest that convenience has bitten me more often than it helped... it makes things certain things implicit that maybe should be explicit as it gives you a dangerous impression that it just works correctly but you might be losing data in the long run:

```python
In [1]: from rdflib import URIRef, Literal
INFO:rdflib:RDFLib Version: 4.2.2-dev

In [2]: import json

In [3]: json.dumps([URIRef('http://foo.bar'), Literal('http://foo.bar')])
Out[3]: '["http://foo.bar", "http://foo.bar"]'
```

(The above should actually throw a `TypeError` on serialization to make you think and notice that you'll lose information and maybe should use json-ld, but as the default `json` is "nice enough" to handle every subclass of `basestring`, it will happily serialize destroying information.)

I know that `rdflib` goes to quite some lengths to make RDF feel pythonic, but quoting from `import this` "Explicit is better than implicit.". Maybe it's worth discussing whether we should just move away from the `unicode` base class to force developers to make explicit decisions where necessary and remove a whole lot of inconsistencies and traps, of which the `Namespace.title` is just one instance?

If you have the time, that'd be a great way to check the implementation, as well as our comparative understandings and needs. (Most things are governed by the spec, but some might be controllable using parameters.)

My suggestion is to always use utf8 at least with the backend operations.

Thanks,
-Vlad

The synchronous list maintenance is different from maintaining cluster consensus in Cassandra, et al. as Patroni delegates the consensus problem to DCS. Externalizing the consensus allows us to be quite flexible in what constitutes a consensus. We just have to follow ordering constraints to ensure state stored in DCS is always conservative/pessimistic. If increasing redundancy (going from 1 of 2 quorum to 1 of 3 quorum) then replicate first, publish later, if increasing number of candidates (from 1 of 2 to 2 of 3 quorum) then publish first, replicate later.

To put it more formally, given a master with `synchronous_stanby_names = ANY k (SyncStandbys)`, `SyncSet = SyncStandbys ∪ {master}`, `replication_factor=k+1`, and DCS state of `any quorum_size of CandidateSet`, then at any point the following invariant must hold:

* `|CandidateSet ∪ SyncSet| < replication_factor + quorum_size ` => any replication set and any quorum set of specified sizes will have at least one overlap.

The user interface for this would generalize nicely over what we have now. The user would have to pick 2 things:

* Maximum number of nodes that can fail at once before quorum is lost.
* Minimum replication factor.

I haven't yet figured out how to migrate current configs over is still unclear, but the following mapping from current settings seems to make sense:

* synchronous_mode=true, synchronous_mode_strict=false -> max_fail=1, min_replication_factor=0
* synchronous_mode=true, synchronous_mode_strict=true -> max_fail=1, min_replication_factor=1

Defaulting to the least harmful default value seems a reasonable choice. But I can understand that one may want to have a mode where errors pop up when a variable is missing, but the user should have the option to choose the first behavior (I just saw that you rejected that option and I do not need it). 
 - I agree on the consensus on `base_function` and defaulting to the simulation period.
 - I greatly suggest that the time-based inference should be provided as an option per variable.
 - A backstop date or period of time might also be implemented. The users may still have problems if they install something that has the same dependencies with different versions.

This problem can be solved in many ways:
- we can bundle manticore along with dependencies using e.g. pyinstaller - this lets us create single binary file containing manticore and dependencies; however this is not that good as it doesn't bundle e.g. libc. Because of that a package built on latest ubuntu may not work on old ubuntu because of glibc version mismatch (afaiu it is forward compatible but not backward compatible)
- virtualenv - this was recommended for a long time in Python communities and so a lot of ppl know it [along with virtualenvwrapper which makes it super easy to use]
- [pipenv](https://github.com/pypa/pipenv) - this is a way of handling dependencies for projects, so I am not sure if this is suitable here;
- some virtualenv bundler - e.g. https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex - someone recommended me this, but I haven't used it

My guess was to structure exception classes in 2 main categories: (a) expected and well handled, and (b) other (like rare, hard to test and not foreseen).

(a) Should provide a short a clear message in the log (and possible the GUI). A stack trace is not included by default, because the message is complete with context.
(b) Should include the stack trace, because the context is quite likely not understood.

If an exception is raise by a module and passed to the main loop, this would allow quite simply to adjust the amount of information per exception category to be printed by catching these 2 exception super classes. 

E.g.: An malformed XML metadata file will cause pysaml2 to print a stack trace, but not the offending metadata source. The stacktrace will point to the plugin, but it is a lot of noise for the short information that metadatafile idp.xml is not well-formed xml in line 5 pos 33. I would say that an invalid metadata file is a well understood and expected error, and error messages should talk to system admins (who might not be SAML experts) as well as developers.

> I imagine an advantage of the Marionette subview approach is that if for example one user's name were to change, only a single <li> DOM node is updated. (Do you know if this is the case?)

Marionette 2.4.5 only seems to re-render automatically under some circumstances when you use the CollectionView. For the CollectionView I would indeed expect it to only re-render the items changed (though I might also expect it to re-render the item's entire template and not just any particular DOM nodes that happened to change). In all other cases it's up to the user to trigger a re-render, and I've done so here pretty aggressively. I re-render the whole app when *any* data changes. This makes the re-rendering logic nearly trivial, which I think is an advantage. Obviously this might not be such a hot idea unless we're using a virtual DOM, if the app becomes very large.

The alternative to aggressive re-rendering + vDOM is to let each view carefully manage when its subviews are re-rendered. The drawback here is potentially complex re-rendering logic. I haven't yet played with integrating some virtual DOM implementation with Marionette, so I can't comment on the relative merits of either approach.

Looking forward to see if enabling the gateway ports on the SAS machine lets this work as expected. Also, I still don't think there's an issue with 'localhost'. It is ssh that is forwarding this extra socket connection over it's already established channel. Given ssh -R 32701:localhost:32702 (changed the ports to be different for clarity), saspy opens and accepts a connection on localhost:32701. It's ssh on that machine that will be connecting to that, on localhost, not the SAS process from the remote machine. And, on the remote machine, it's ssh that opens and accepts localhost:32702, and the filename statement in SAS (that saspy sent) connects to that localhost:32702 (the local port forwarded over the ssh tunnel to localhost:32701 on the saspy side by ssh). Neither saspy nor SAS are aware that their 'localhost' connections are being forwarded off-host for them.
So, it is the loopback interface on each machine that is the correct connection for each side. Only ssh knows that it's forwarding the socket from one host to the other.
That's the way I understand this, and I could be wrong. But I believe this is correct and localhost is right on both machines.

I've seen this happen myself. In our case, one of the nodes ran out of memory and froze, which made EC2s health checks fail. The auto scaling group killed it, and a new instance took it's place. I also scaled the cluter up to 5 when that happened, just so it wouldn't happen again.

In our case, `deis-database` was running on the unhealthy node. When `deis-database` starts up, it attempts to recover it's previous state from `deis-store`. Very fair, but unfortionally `deis-store` ended up in a corrupt state where one of the database backups reported a size of 0 instead of <whatever it's supposed to be>. To fix it, I had to use `ceph` commands to remove the dead node. This caused `deis-store` to do some automatic recovery things and after about 5 minutes and some restarts of `deis-database` and `deis-controller` our cluster was up and running again.

I was able to get the ceph status using `ceph -s`. I'm a bit unsure why you couldn't get it. Did you try from multiple hosts?

If `deis scale` hangs thats most likely due to a dead `deis-controller`.

To avoid it in the future, well, eh...You need to have some server health monitoring running. Unfortionally, I have yet to find one solution which gives the best possible solution for CoreOS (since CoreOS is very Docker focused, and Docker isn't the easiest thing to monitor because of it's daemon process model). The best solution I've found so far is Datadog. Ensure that you always have memory available, even if one host goes down and all non-global units (e.g. your applications and most deis components) needs to be rescheduled to the remaining two servers.

Yeah, I think we have the Three Laws of Conda Update:

1. `conda update` will not make the environment inconsistent or, through inaction, allow an environment to become inconsistent.
2. `conda update` will install the packages explicitly requested by the user on the command line, except when it conflicts with the First Law.
3. `conda update` will preserve the package state of environment, except when it conflicts with the First or Second Law.

Generic absolute symlinks within conda packages might be a tough sell. It really seems to violate one of conda's code portability principals. Shadow packages however, are something we've needed for a while. Not only could they work for stuff conda will never install, but also for packages other installers (like pip) install into an environment.

And for completeness, the idea of using a prefix naming convention, as I've already been doing. With this, we'd have `r-bokeh`:

**Advantages:**

- Requires absolutely no changes to conda at all. 

**Disadvantages:**

- Not very user-friendly, compared to what I am suggesting. We could possibly make the conda CLI know about prefix conventions (or at least the most common ones) and provide a nicer wrapper on them. So, e.g., conda install `nloptr` (instead of `r-nloptr`) "just works", and `conda install bokeh` asks which bokeh (again with the potential to configure "always python" or whatever in the .condarc).  However, any CLI command that can't receive user interaction (like `--yes` or `--json`) would have no choice but to only use literal package names, which could potentially lead to some confusion. 

  It's also worth noting that the existing user experience is not too bad unless there is a name conflict, because conda will suggest name corrections automatically:

```
# conda install nloptr
Fetching package metadata: ...
Error: No packages found in current linux-64 channels matching: nloptr

Did you mean one of these?
```

The use case of the snapshots is "something got fucked up, I want to roll back to before the fuckup". But if the fuckup happened 1 hour ago, you'll restore a different snapshot than if it happened 5 days ago (i.e. most recent not-broken data). So you want to have a high density of snapshots that spans back for quite some time.

Because lots snapshots hardly give (disk) overhead (i.e. because of Lucene segments), the simple but stupid approach was to store shitloads of snapshots. That is however out of the question because it makes the entire ES cluster slow whenever a snapshot is started (which, being every 20 min, basically means the entire time).

So I need to find a way to *keep* generations of snapshots easily. I can't hardcode to exclude a certain snapshot because the relevancy is subject to a sliding window. That would require a wrapper that lists all snapshots and then finds out which ones to keep, ergo what to pass to `--exclude`.

I understand your suggestion, but it seems more convenient to put this into a parameter instead of a wrapper around `--exclude`. Just like there is `--disk-space` which recursively "keeps" snapshots until a certain counter is out of bounds?

The Elasticsearch API does not allow for this, so Curator does not either.  The only way to accomplish this would be to get all repositories, and iterate over them.  This _could_ be done using the Curator API, though it would be unwieldy.

I would invite you to consider re-thinking your backup strategy a bit.  Snapshots are not the same as other incremental backups.

Snapshots are incremental at the _segment_ level, not at the file or document level.  When a snapshot is taken, it backs up every _new_ segment it has no record of, and points to _every segment already snapshotted._ No segment is ever deleted as long as a single snapshot points to it. This means that every single snapshot is like a full snapshot, because it points to every segment necessary to restore the cluster to that point in time. You shouldn't need to have a new repository every week.  You could just do weekly snapshots and those would be your full "restore to that point in time" backups.  You could delete _any_ daily snapshots in between these weekly snapshots and that would cover things for you.  Just name them differently (you could prefix them with `daily-` and `weekly-`) so they are easily found and pruned by Curator.  You could then keep daily incremental snapshots as long as you deemed necessary, and weekly ones at a different retention.

@AutorestCI rebuild https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/blob/master/specification/authorization/resource-manager/readme.md

Capabilities and security policy features.

Also renamed several types and operations for improved clarify and
consistency.

Additions:

- BlobAuditingPolicy APIs (e.g. `databases.create_or_update_blob_auditing_policy`)
- ThreatDetectionPolicy APIs (e.g. `databases.create_or_update_threat_detection_policy`)
- databases.list_by_server now supports $expand parameter
- Capabilities APIs (e.g. capabilities.list_by_location)

Classes and enums renamed:

- ServerFirewallRule -> FirewallRule
- DatabaseEditions -> DatabaseEdition
- ElasticPoolEditions -> ElasticPoolEdition
- ImportRequestParameters -> ImportRequest
- ExportRequestParameters -> ExportRequest
- ImportExportOperationResponse -> ImportExportResponse
- OperationMode -> ImportOperationMode
- TransparentDataEncryptionStates -> TransparentDataEncryptionStatus

Classes removed:

- Unused types: UpgradeHint, Schema, Table, Column

Operations renamed:

- servers.get_by_resource_group -> servers.get
- servers.create_or_update_firewall_rule -> firewall_rules.create_or_update, and
similar for get, list, and delete
- databases.import -> databases.create_import_operation
- servers.import -> databases.import
- databases.pause_data_warehouse -> databases.pause
- databases.resume_data_warehouse -> databases.resume
- recommended_elastic_pools.list -> recommended_elastic_pools.list_by_server

Operations removed:

- Removed ImportExport operation results APIs since these are handled
  automatically by Azure async pattern.

>Can you elucidate more on what defining them in markdown cells breaks for conversion to latex with nbconvert?

In a regular latex document, one typically defines macros outside of any math environment; the macros are then in effect throughout the document.  In the jupyter notebook (though I think this is a mathjax "feature") one instead defines macros inside a math environment (so that the parser picks them up as a math thing, I guess) and they are somehow made to have effect throughout the notebook.

When nbconvert converts a notebook to latex, it (naturally) leaves the macros inside a math environment.  But in the latex document, that means the macros take effect **only within that particular math environment; i.e., between the surrounding delimiters only**.

That's the main problem when dealing with a single notebook.  One may fix it manually by removing the math delimiters around sets of macros in the .tex file.  

When dealing with a set of notebooks that will be merged into a single .tex document, the workaround just mention fails, since repeated definitions of the same macro are not allowed (and one would typically redefine certain macros in multiple notebooks).

I've also tried using an nbconvert template to insert my macros, but again I get errors because the macros are redefined by the commands copied from the notebook.

Yeah I can see why this is confusing. The `grad` function has only supported this kind of broadcasting since a [recent commit](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/commit/fabac3f896de86f255fa7dbf6ede51192a24baa3), and I think we may not have properly documented it yet.

When you do
```python
fgrad(0.0, x)
```
you're getting the _sum_ of all the derivatives of the elements of f(0.0, x) w.r.t. its first argument: 

2 * 0. + 2 * 0.2 + ... + 2 * 2. = 11.

This behaviour ought to be explained in the tutorial.

I think I understand the issue finally.

The function I'm analyzing is a resource loader.  So the switch is "which resource", and in almost every case it opens a file, and then ends by closing the file if it's opened.  However, there are one or two cases where it instead instantiates a specialty class to handle it.

Therefore, the function control flow looks like:
```
  switch node -> case 1    -> file close block  -> return block
                      -> case 2    -> file close block  -> return block
                      -> case 3    -> file close block  -> return block
                      ...
                      -> case n                                   -> return block
```
The method just uses the same file close code for each case.  The writer, when working on the next case, thinks it's visited when it hasn't necessarily.

I don't know how to code it up in Java, goto statements would be involved.  When I copy and paste, the compiler isn't smart enough to optimize it all into one block.  Perhaps smali is required for a test case.  Or there's a pattern I'm unfamiliar with.

Thus, using the return block for the follow node IS the correct behavior.  The writer, however, will need to be modified to handle this situation so it doesn't skip showing code and lose the semantic information.

You're right that attributes and parent/child relationships don't mix very well. Stuff you can do out-of-the-box that doesn't make a lot of sense:

* You can add conflicting attributes to both the parent and a child. Which is correct?
* You can have two identical products, but one has a bunch of attributes on the parent, while others have a bunch on the children. Because there is no canonical representation, it makes comparing products and finding duplicates tricky

You're also right that it's confusing to be able to have a standalone product, as well as having a product with a single child. These are functionally identical, but again, there's no canonical representation.

I think the rationale for having the two separate structures is that if you have standalone products, it's straightforward to have an "add to basket" button directly on the product thumbnail (in search results or whatever), and while it's technically possible to have an "add to basket" button on a parent with a single child, the logic for checking that can be somewhat tricky.

Although I agree in principle, I don't think this would be a scenario we would run in real life. The key formats are standardized, and the underlying library developers have a (likely bigger) group of people providing support for these formats. @nbonnotte OK, I'm going to reopen this to look into it, but I think both modes are *defensible*, and when both are defensible I think "don't change anything" tends to rule out so that we're not changing behavior that people may be relying on.

I'm not sure why you want to check the time zone of the parsed date, though. If you just want the time zone in UTC, you don't actually need to check if it's already in UTC, you can just do this:

```
from dateutil import tz
dt_in_utc = dt.astimezone(tz.utc())
```

Then there's no ambiguity - it's in UTC. If you have some control structure that depends on whether or not this datetime was originally represented in UTC, I don't know that there's a non-convoluted way to do it, though there are many simple ways to do it depending on what edge cases you need to account for. You should be able to just check that `dt.tzinfo.utcoffset() == 0`, which would work unless you care about differentiating between UTC and something like WET, which is UTC+0 sometimes, but transitions to WEST, UTC+1 during the summer. This would only come up if your application runs on computers representing timestamps like `2015-06-01 12:04:33 WET` and their local time zone is set to `WET` or `WEST`.

In any case, I think it may be a separate issue, but I will look into the possibility of modifying `tz.tzlocal` such that if it is effectively UTC that it compares equal to `tz.tzutc`.

This behaviour looks good to me.

However, I would do the test in `test_deserialization.py`, in `test_strict_integer_field_deserialization` or another test function close to it.

(I would put those tests in `test_fields.py` but since the other `strict` tests are in `test_deserialization.py`, let's put all of them together. There must be a good reason.)

Regarding the deserialization vs. serialization, the rationale is that we want to validate what gets in our application, so we validate on deserialization.

IIUC, the fact that the `_format_num` function, which is called on both serialization and deserialization, does validation is more an accident that a design principle. We don't really want to validate our outputs as we assume our own code is safe. Therefore, I wouldn't test serialization, as we might remove this validation in the future without breaking the API contract.

There are places where validation is done on serialization, but the trend is to remove them (see Email and URL fields here: https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/commit/3c649b92af52e1559b3b1cf52cf3b0aedb3c756c).

Just my humble opinion.

`get_or_create` fails only if there is no info about `money` field:

Good:
```python
{'money': Money(0, 'EUR')}
```

Also good:

```python
{'money': 10, 'money_currency': 'PLN'}
```

Bad:
```
{'money_currency': 'PLN'}
```

Now we manipulate with currency if currency is absent - we're setting default value for it.
Probably we should consider opposite situation, when we have only currency and no amount. We can just take default value for amount.
Something like this (`models.managers.py`):

```python
from .fields import MoneyField, CurrencyField

...

def _expand_money_kwargs(model, args=(), kwargs=None, exclusions=()):
    """
    Augments kwargs so that they contain _currency lookups.
    """
    involved_fields = [_get_clean_name(name) for name in kwargs]
    for name, value in list(kwargs.items()):
        if name in exclusions:
            continue
        if isinstance(value, Money):
            clean_name = _get_clean_name(name)
            kwargs[name] = value.amount
            kwargs[get_currency_field_name(clean_name)] = smart_unicode(value.currency)
        else:
            field = _get_field(model, name)
            if isinstance(field, MoneyField):
                if isinstance(value, (BaseExpression, F)):
                    clean_name = _get_clean_name(name)
                    if not isinstance(value, F):
                        value = prepare_expression(value)
                    kwargs[get_currency_field_name(clean_name)] = F(get_currency_field_name(value.name))
                if is_in_lookup(name, value):
                    args += (_convert_in_lookup(model, name, value), )
                    del kwargs[name]
            elif isinstance(field, CurrencyField):
                money_field_name = name[:-9]  # Remove '_currency'
                if money_field_name not in involved_fields:
                    money_field = _get_field(model, money_field_name)
                    kwargs[money_field_name] = money_field.default.amount

    return args, kwargs
```

The first stage of the Plan for maintenance is more clear now, so here's a heads up!

## Different repos

All the Graphene integrations are now separated into different repos. The docs are also in each repo, so maintaining and updating them is much easier now! :)

## Transparency and Core Contributors

As the integrations are now separated from the core, is easier to achieve better project permissions.
This integrations will have more transparency and core maintainers apart of myself. I have few people in mind for each of the integrations (excluding the ones that are already maintained and created by other people, like `graphene-gae` and `graphene-peewee`).

## Project priorities

Also, for the main repository, `graphene` we will take advantage of the new feature of Github -[Projects](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/projects)- and use it as board of the priorities for the project. Any idea will be discussed and we will decide as community what are the features we want graphene to have, while preserving compatibility and assuring a good path for the future!

@kavink You can query the database for a condition, but you don't really need to.  That is what you can use these classes for.  Your mutations will usually be tied to updating a database.  Just publish each mutation to a channel (inside your mutation resolver), after you write a mutation to your database and before it returns, and then any subscriptions subscribed to that channel (in their resolver) will automatically be updated.  See this [article](https://dev-blog.apollodata.com/graphql-subscriptions-in-apollo-client-9a2457f015fb) for some good background on this concept.  Probably move this to another issue (and not this pull request) if you need further discussion.

I had the same problem and I used the following workaround:

- Use a standalone `enum.Enum` as the reference enum type (no inline definition)
- Wrap into a SQLAlchemy enum: `sqlalchemy.Enum(SexType, name='SexType’)`
- Reference the SQLAlchemy in the model
- Create graphene mappings (`graphene.Enum.from_enum` doesn’t work with SQLAlchemy):

```
SexType = graphene.Enum('SexType', model.SexType.__members__.items())

(...)
@convert_sqlalchemy_type.register(sqlalchemy.types.Enum)
def convert_enum_to_enum(type, column, registry=None):
    if type.name == 'SexType':
        return graphene.Field(
            SexType,
            description=getattr(column, 'doc', None),
            required=not(getattr(column, 'nullable', True)),
        )
    (...)

```

As discussed, I've fixed the appveyor build by interpreting the given string as a binary PSK and changed to implicit base32 padding.
Currently only the travis test fails due to unacceptable PSK, but for this a decision need to be made how to handle CLI and binary PSK. I could do it as with otNoteApi and just use the string as the PSKd, but this would neglect the issue that it's impossible to pass true binary PSKd via the CLI and also the encode/decode would not be used anywhere except in the tests.

I've also took a look at different definitions that I mentioned before and some more issues arose.

First, there are three definitions regarding the PSK - `OPENTHREAD_PSK_MAX_LENGTH`, `OT_PSKD_MAX_SIZE` and `Dtls::kPskMaxLength`. My suggestion to use and interpret them as follows:
* `OPENTHREAD_PSK_MAX_LENGTH` - use as a joiner credential length, i.e. 32 symbols. This should probaly be renamed to be less vague, for example OT_JOINER_CREDENTIAL_MAX_SIZE and moved from windows into openthread headers.
* `OT_PSKD_MAX_SIZE` - use as a PSKd length, i.e. 20 bytes, this would replace kPskMaxLength in commisioner and joiner code.
* `Dtls::kPskMaxLength` - use as a generic DTLS PSK length, i.e. according to RFC4279 it should be 64 octets.

Second, NCP spinel and windows ioctl pass PSKd as a character string, so it would be good to have some feedback from someone who's responsible for those parts, namely in what format should the key be passed - as a string or a binary array and length. Seeming that this PR interferes with different sections of OpenThread I would not want it to break it.

Finally, somewhat unrelated to this PR, but more of a general concern is the 6 byte joiner credential minimum length. First thing is that 6 base32 characters do not correspond to whole number of binary bytes, i.e. 6*5bits/8bits = 3.75 bytes. Second, in case of a implicit-padding base32 encoding it's impossible to have 6 character string, i.e. 3 bytes are encoded in 5 characters and 4 bytes are encoded in 7, therefore skipping 6 characters altogether. Third, having 30bit PSK would constitute about 10^9 different keys and it is reasonable to expect of a non-govermental party capable of checking 10^5 passwords per second, which gives less than three hours of brute-force attack. Not to mention that a well-funded could to orders of magnitude faster attacks. I doubt this will have any effect as the Thread spec is out, but I would highly suggest increasing the minimum allowed joiner credential length.

I see -- I was thinking about this too recently -- I was trying to make pybind11 disambiguate between overloads on `array_t<SimpleStruct>` and `array_t<PackedStruct>` and it seemed impossible with the current machinery (it would always call the first one defined no matter what, with forcecast off). This is pretty bad since sometimes you want to be sure the data is *not* copied if the function's objective is to mutate it. The only way to achieve that would be through a `NPY_NO_CASTING` check. Also, I don't think  `forcecast` is a proper solution for this at all.

What we can do, I think, is expose casting rules:

```rst
.. c:type:: NPY_CASTING

    .. versionadded:: 1.6

    An enumeration type indicating how permissive data conversions should
    be. This is used by the iterator added in NumPy 1.6, and is intended
    to be used more broadly in a future version.

    .. c:var:: NPY_NO_CASTING

        Only allow identical types.

    .. c:var:: NPY_EQUIV_CASTING

       Allow identical and casts involving byte swapping.

    .. c:var:: NPY_SAFE_CASTING

       Only allow casts which will not cause values to be rounded,
       truncated, or otherwise changed.

    .. c:var:: NPY_SAME_KIND_CASTING

       Allow any safe casts, and casts between types of the same kind.
       For example, float64 -> float32 is permitted with this rule.

    .. c:var:: NPY_UNSAFE_CASTING

       Allow any cast, no matter what kind of data loss may occur.
```

So, in `array_t::ensure` we could first check if what we're receiving as an argument is a NumPy array, if it is, we can first do a cast check according to a casting rule (`PyArray_CanCastTypeTo`).

This could be set to safe casting by default, which would rule out casting from structs to scalars or floats to ints and the like.

One more point -- the `NPY_NO_CASTING` flag only makes sense, I think, if the input object is already an ndarray (and not a Python list or whatnot). Setting this flag plus the `NPY_CONTIGUOUS` would pretty much ensure you're not getting a copy of the array and you can mutate the original.

I found that I had to uninstall python-social-auth and `rm -rf site-packages/social` because the uninstall leaves a migration file that conflicts. After that installing `0.2.21` and running the migrations worked for me.

I finally solved the problem!
My `pip` and `sudo pip` point to different locations.
Therefore, I use `sudo env 'PATH=$PATH:<loaction>' pip` to upgrade the version of `social-auth-app-django`, and then the fb api is working now!
Thank you very much!

At the ODC conference last week there was a lot of interest in removing the barrier of index/ingesting data for new users. We're working on documentation/script/data updates to hopefully be released in the next few weeks.

Oh, I see, I missed that you were using an `Instrument` to keep a shadow table to track the list of tasks. The other approach is to pull it out of trio on the fly (the `print-task-tree.py` file shows how). The benefit of keeping the shadow table is that you can print a list of tasks, and potentially which one is running currently, even when the run loop is locked up and non-reponsive. The downside is that you need to know ahead of time that you're going to have a problem and set up the Instrument, and it adds some overhead during normal execution.

The other approach is to wait until someone actually connects to the monitor before doing anything. This is nice because it doesn't add any overhead, so you can leave your production app running in this mode and then connect after things have gone wrong. The downside is that if things have *really* gone wrong, you might not be able to connect...

> open yourself up to the Werkzeug debugger attack if you listen on a remotely accessible socket

Oh for sure you don't want to allow untrusted connections to a debug interface like this. But "trusted" and "local" aren't necessarily the same. You could have a policy that even local users need to provide some authentication before connecting to the debug interface. Or you could be in a situation where your program is running in a container environment and you have to export a regular tcp socket to access it at all (presumably locking it down somehow in the process). Or maybe you really want to access it remotely and are taking appropriate precautions, like requiring an SSL client certificate or ssh authentication. (The twisted "manhole" interface supports a mode where you connect using ssh.) If the core interface just takes a `Stream`, then it's straightforward to build all these things on top. And of course we'd still want to provide some helpers with secure defaults.

Thinking about it, we might be able to make the task tree dumping work from other threads, without actually entering the trio thread. Most operations would be to be run on the thread though.

So maybe a best-of-all-worlds approach would be to make the monitor use trio for power/flexibility, and design it so that it can run either in the main thread or in a secondary thread. This shouldn't be terribly difficult; basically it means routing operations through a portal object, which could either send them over to the main thread or else run them directly. And for a few operations that we particularly want to work even if the main thread is stuck, we can do some special case handling.

This way it'd even be possible to detect when our attempt to enter the main thread had gotten stuck, and print a stack dump.

Given the descriptions above, `trio.plumbing` would make sense to me as someone relatively unfamiliar with the details of the Trio API. Even without the git precedent, the underlying analogy of "If you're just using the bathroom, you don't need to care about this layer, but if you're building a bathroom, or water is unexpectedly pouring over the bathroom floor, this layer is important" is a good one.

I'm afraid we're not going to do that, because like I said above, whether that's appropriate depends on the higher-level protocol and how the socket is being used, and the vast majority of programs do not use that pattern. It's much easier for the occasional code that wants that to implement it themselves on top of our generic API, than for all the codes that don't want it to always check for whether they have a `SocketStream` and if so call `aclose_forcefully`.

While both write_data interfaces say they take file-like objects you can see in the schemaless_writer for CPython it converts the filelike object to a byte array and then back again.  schemaless_writer however has the same signature for CPython and PyPy3.

https://github.com/fastavro/fastavro/blob/ae2a19fd01f26b22f37fcf6eed586bd1aeadedcb/fastavro/_write.pyx#L718

https://github.com/fastavro/fastavro/blob/ae2a19fd01f26b22f37fcf6eed586bd1aeadedcb/fastavro/_write_py.py#L602
See #40 for the internal API redefinition. Once this is done, it should be simpler to fix this one.
